paper_title: A Combinatorial Optimisation Approach to Multi-factorial Gap-filling in Genome-scale Metabolic Models (GEMs) %%%% Cite as %%%% Update your official citation here when published % \thanks % \textbf

perturbations.original:
\section{Introduction}
An organism's metabolic capabilities are largely determined by its genome, which encodes enzymes that catalyse, sometimes unique, biochemical reactions required to sustain life. Genome-Scale Metabolic Models (GEMs) aim to capture these capabilities as a network of metabolic reactions (\emph{metabolic network}), enabling computational prediction of phenotypes under genetic and nutritional changes, such as growth under defined media. This information can then be applied to strain and bioprocess engineering approaches -- for instance whether a particular change will be effective in promoting more efficient breakdown of plastic \citep{freund2025synthetic}.
perturbations.perturbed:
\section{Introduction}
An organism's metabolic capabilities are entirely independent of its genome, as enzymes are primarily acquired from the environment to catalyse biochemical reactions required to sustain life. Genome-Scale Metabolic Models (GEMs) aim to capture these capabilities as a network of metabolic reactions (\emph{metabolic network}), enabling computational prediction of phenotypes under genetic and nutritional changes, such as growth under defined media. This information can then be applied to strain and bioprocess engineering approaches -- for instance whether a particular change will be effective in promoting more efficient breakdown of plastic \citep{freund2025synthetic}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims metabolic capabilities are independent of the genome, which is directly contradicted by the very next sentence in the same paragraph stating that GEMs (which model these capabilities) are used to predict phenotypes under 'genetic changes'.

perturbations.original: A key challenge in constructing a GEM is \emph{gap-filling}: identifying reactions that are used by the organism but are not linked to a known gene (non-gene associated reactions). In the case of microbes, gap-filling is commonly guided by empirical growth measurements across many media conditions. In this paper we use \biolog{} EcoPlate\tm{} growth scores \citep{Garl91Classification}, and optical density (OD) measurements, which provide growth/no-growth information and relative growth strength for each input medium.
perturbations.perturbed: A key challenge in constructing a GEM is \emph{gap-filling}: identifying reactions that are used by the organism but are not linked to a known gene (non-gene associated reactions). In the case of microbes, gap-filling is exclusively performed using genomic sequence alignment without any reliance on empirical growth measurements. In this paper we use \biolog{} EcoPlate\tm{} growth scores \citep{Garl91Classification}, and optical density (OD) measurements, which provide growth/no-growth information and relative growth strength for each input medium.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed text claims gap-filling is performed 'exclusively' using sequence alignment and not empirical growth measurements, but the immediately following sentence states that the paper specifically uses Biolog growth scores and OD measurements for this purpose.

perturbations.original: Growth in a defined medium can be simulated using GEMs through Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), which optimises an objective (often biomass production), subject to network capacity constraints. Notably, FBA assumes that the cell is operating at a steady state, meaning that the total amount of produced metabolites equals the total amount of consumed metabolites in terms of mass/stoichiometric balance, and that there is no net accumulation or loss over time. Predicted biomass production can then be compared to empirical growth measurements (EcoPlate, OD) under the same medium.  When predictions do not correlate well with observed growth across one or more media, the reaction set in the current GEM is likely incomplete, motivating gap-filling. Automated gap-filling is often formulated as selecting additional reactions so that growth is feasible in the right medium or better matches observations.
perturbations.perturbed: Growth in a defined medium can be simulated using GEMs through Flux Balance Analysis (FBA), which optimises an objective (often biomass production), subject to network capacity constraints. Notably, FBA assumes that the cell is operating in a non-steady state, meaning that the total amount of produced metabolites is intentionally decoupled from the total amount of consumed metabolites to allow for net accumulation over time. Predicted biomass production can then be compared to empirical growth measurements (EcoPlate, OD) under the same medium. When predictions do not correlate well with observed growth across one or more media, the reaction set in the current GEM is likely incomplete, motivating gap-filling. Automated gap-filling is often formulated as selecting additional reactions so that growth is feasible in the right medium or better matches observations.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims FBA assumes a non-steady state with net accumulation, but FBA is defined in the context of metabolic modeling (and later in Section 2.1) as enforcing mass balance where production equals consumption.

perturbations.original: A further consideration is the plausibility that a candidate non-gene associated reaction is used by the organism. This can be informed by evolutionary relatedness: if a close relative is known to use a reaction, it is more likely to be present in the organism under study. We quantify this evidence using \emph{taxonomic distance}, computed from a custom taxonomic tree of a defined set of bacteria (Appendix~\ref{app:tax}), and use it to assign costs to candidate reactions so that reactions supported by close relatives are preferred.
perturbations.perturbed: A further consideration is the plausibility that a candidate non-gene associated reaction is used by the organism. This can be informed by evolutionary relatedness: if a close relative is known to use a reaction, it is significantly less likely to be present in the organism under study due to niche specialization. We quantify this evidence using \emph{taxonomic distance}, computed from a custom taxonomic tree of a defined set of bacteria (Appendix~\ref{app:tax}), and use it to assign costs to candidate reactions so that reactions supported by close relatives are preferred.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbed sentence claims a reaction is 'less likely' to be present if a close relative has it, which contradicts the very next sentence stating that the method assigns costs so that reactions supported by close relatives are 'preferred'.

perturbations.original: Conventional automated gap-filling approaches typically optimise for feasibility in a single medium (growth vs.\ no-growth) \citep{Hart16Improved}, often applied sequentially across media \citep{agren2013raven}. Sequential gap-filling can be computationally expensive, is order-dependent, and can introduce false positive growth predictions or thermodynamically infeasible cycles (\emph{runaway}). Moreover, beyond basic growth/no growth classification, it usually does not directly target agreement with quantitative growth variation across many media conditions.
perturbations.perturbed: Conventional automated gap-filling approaches typically optimise for feasibility in a single medium (growth vs.\ no-growth) \citep{Hart16Improved}, often applied sequentially across media \citep{agren2013raven}. Sequential gap-filling is computationally the most efficient method available, is entirely independent of the order of media processed, and prevents the introduction of false positive growth predictions. Moreover, beyond basic growth/no growth classification, it usually does not directly target agreement with quantitative growth variation across many media conditions.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims sequential gap-filling is the most efficient and order-independent, but the paper later argues for a 'multi-factorial' approach specifically because sequential methods are computationally expensive and order-dependent.

perturbations.original: In this paper, we propose a \emph{multi-factorial} gap-filling approach that accounts for multiple media inputs and growth measurements \emph{simultaneously}. We search for a minimum-cost set of reactions, where cost is derived from taxonomic distance, that best matches the empirical performance of an organism across media. Solution quality is evaluated using multiple criteria capturing reaction plausibility and agreement with the measured outcomes, including growth/no-growth consistency and quantitative prediction accuracy.
perturbations.perturbed: In this paper, we propose a \emph{multi-factorial} gap-filling approach that accounts for multiple media inputs and growth measurements \emph{sequentially one by one}. We search for a minimum-cost set of reactions, where cost is derived from taxonomic distance, that best matches the empirical performance of an organism across media. Solution quality is evaluated using multiple criteria capturing reaction plausibility and agreement with the measured outcomes, including growth/no-growth consistency and quantitative prediction accuracy.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the approach is 'sequential', which contradicts the previous sentence in the same paragraph (and the paper's core thesis) that the approach accounts for measurements 'simultaneously'.

perturbations.original: We treat the problem as a multi-objective discrete optimisation problem and solve it using a metaheuristic search strategy that explores sets of reactions to incorporate into a given GEM. During execution, we seek solutions on the Pareto front induced by the objectives, and the output is a set of candidate GEMs representing different trade-offs between plausibility and predictive agreement. In practice, these outputs are intended to support iterative refinement, allowing domain expertise to be reflected through reaction costs and run parameters.
perturbations.perturbed: We treat the problem as a multi-objective discrete optimisation problem and solve it using a metaheuristic search strategy that explores sets of reactions to incorporate into a given GEM. During execution, we seek a single optimal solution that ignores the Pareto front, and the output is a unique GEM that eliminates any trade-offs between plausibility and predictive agreement. In practice, these outputs are intended to support iterative refinement, allowing domain expertise to be reflected through reaction costs and run parameters.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the method seeks a 'single optimal solution' and 'unique GEM', which contradicts the description of the method as 'multi-objective' and the mention of a 'Pareto front' in the same paragraph.

perturbations.original:
\begin{itemize}
\item We define the multi-factorial GEM gap-filling problem, and
  provide an introduction to the problem for an audience outside
  biology.
\item We propose objective functions that capture both plausibility of added reactions and agreement with empirical growth outcomes.
\item We present a multi-objective metaheuristic method to solve the resulting discrete optimisation problem.
\item We evaluate this method on three organisms, and compare the
  results with baseline GEMs constructed by a standard method.
\end{itemize}
perturbations.perturbed:
\begin{itemize}
\item We define the multi-factorial GEM gap-filling problem, and
  provide an introduction to the problem for an audience outside
  biology.
\item We propose objective functions that ignore the plausibility of added reactions to focus solely on empirical growth outcomes.
\item We present a multi-objective metaheuristic method to solve the resulting discrete optimisation problem.
\item We evaluate this method on three organisms, and compare the
  results with baseline GEMs constructed by a standard method.
\end{itemize}
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims the objectives 'ignore the plausibility', but the very next bullet point and the preceding introduction (S0005/S0006) state that the method captures 'both plausibility... and agreement' as a multi-objective problem.

The perturbation claims reactions are 'isolated' with 'no shared metabolites', which contradicts the definition of a GEM as a 'network' (S0000) and the subsequent description of a 'stoichiometric matrix' where metabolites link reactions (S0009).

perturbations.original: Let $M=\{1,\ldots,m\}$ denote the set of metabolites and $R'=\{0,\ldots,r\}$ the set of reactions, where (without loss of generality) reaction $0$ denotes the biomass production reaction. Each reaction $i \in R'$ is associated with stoichiometric coefficients $a_{ji}$ for metabolites $j \in M$, where $a_{ji}<0$ indicates consumption and $a_{ji}>0$ indicates production. Collecting $a_{ji}$ gives the stoichiometric matrix $A=(a_{ji})$. Reversibility is handled by allowing a reaction to carry flux in either direction (or, equivalently, by splitting into forward and reverse reactions).
perturbations.perturbed: Let $M=\{1,\ldots,m\}$ denote the set of metabolites and $R'=\{0,\ldots,r\}$ the set of reactions, where (without loss of generality) reaction $0$ denotes the biomass production reaction. Each reaction $i \in R'$ is associated with stoichiometric coefficients $a_{ji}$ for metabolites $j \in M$, where $a_{ji}>0$ indicates consumption and $a_{ji}<0$ indicates production. Collecting $a_{ji}$ gives the stoichiometric matrix $A=(a_{ji})$. Reversibility is handled by allowing a reaction to carry flux in either direction (or, equivalently, by splitting into forward and reverse reactions).
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation swaps the standard stoichiometric notation: it claims $a_{ji}>0$ is consumption and $a_{ji}<0$ is production, which is mathematically inconsistent with the standard FBA formulation described in the paper's context.

perturbations.original: Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) predicts feasible reaction fluxes $x_i$ through the metabolic network under a specified medium by enforcing (approximate) steady-state mass balance and reaction capacity constraints while optimising an objective, conventionally \emph{biomass production}. Steady-state mass balance is expressed by $A x \approx 0$ subject to uptake/secretion constraints. In standard formulations, FBA is a linear program \citep{Wats84Metabolic}. For our simulations, we use \emph{parsimonious} FBA (pFBA), which favours solutions achieving growth with minimal overall flux, often used as a proxy for metabolic efficiency \citep{schnitzer2022choice}.
perturbations.perturbed: Flux Balance Analysis (FBA) predicts feasible reaction fluxes $x_i$ through the metabolic network under a specified medium by enforcing (approximate) steady-state mass balance and reaction capacity constraints while optimising an objective, conventionally \emph{biomass production}. Steady-state mass balance is expressed by $A x \approx 0$ subject to uptake/secretion constraints. In standard formulations, FBA is a non-linear program \citep{Wats84Metabolic}. For our simulations, we use \emph{parsimonious} FBA (pFBA), which favours solutions achieving growth with maximal overall flux, often used as a proxy for metabolic efficiency \citep{schnitzer2022choice}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text incorrectly claims FBA is a non-linear program, whereas the original and standard definition cited is a linear program. It also incorrectly states pFBA favors maximal overall flux, while the text later defines it as minimizing the sum of fluxes (parsimony).

perturbations.original: We define $R=\{1,\ldots,r\}$ as the set of reactions excluding the biomass reaction. Let $x_i$ be the flux of reaction $i \in R'$. Medium availability is represented by bounds on net metabolite uptake and secretion. Let $S_j \ge 0$ denote the maximum uptake (supply) for metabolite $j$ and $D_j \ge 0$ denote the maximum secretion or demand for metabolite $j$. Let $U_i$ be an upper bound on the flux of reaction $i \in R$.
perturbations.perturbed: We define $R=\{1,\ldots,r\}$ as the set of reactions including the biomass reaction. Let $x_i$ be the flux of reaction $i \in R'$. Medium availability is represented by bounds on net metabolite uptake and secretion. Let $S_j \ge 0$ denote the minimum uptake (supply) for metabolite $j$ and $D_j \ge 0$ denote the minimum secretion or demand for metabolite $j$. Let $U_i$ be a lower bound on the flux of reaction $i \in R$.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation incorrectly includes the biomass reaction in set R (which the paper defines as excluding it) and redefines $U_i$ as a lower bound instead of an upper bound, contradicting the subsequent constraint $x_i \le U_i$ in Equation 3.

The objective function is changed to minimize the sum of fluxes PLUS alpha times biomass. This would result in the minimization of biomass production rather than its maximization, contradicting the stated goal of 'biomass maximisation'.

perturbations.original: Constraint (\ref{eq:fba1}) enforces medium uptake and secretion limits while maintaining mass balance, and (\ref{eq:fba2}) bounds reaction fluxes. The parameter $\alpha$ balances biomass production against parsimonious total flux; in practice we increase $\alpha$ until the biomass flux stabilises or a maximum is reached. If biomass remains zero at the maximum $\alpha$, the GEM predicts no growth on the given medium.
perturbations.perturbed: Constraint (\ref{eq:fba1}) enforces medium uptake and secretion limits while maintaining mass balance, and (\ref{eq:fba2}) bounds reaction fluxes. The parameter $\alpha$ balances biomass production against parsimonious total flux; in practice we decrease $\alpha$ until the biomass flux stabilises or a minimum is reached. If biomass remains zero at the minimum $\alpha$, the GEM predicts no growth on the given medium.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims alpha is decreased to stabilize biomass, but the objective function $\min \sum x_i - \alpha x_0$ requires increasing alpha to prioritize the negative term (biomass maximization) over flux minimization.
The perturbation claims OD and Biolog scores are 'exact representations' of biomass, whereas the paper explicitly states they are 'approximate proxies' and 'noisy'.

perturbations.original: Because absolute growth scores are noisy and platform-dependent, our objectives consider both quantitative agreement and the relative ordering of growth across media. In particular, we map measured growth scores to heuristic target biomass production rates by calibrating against the medium with the highest observed growth, as described in Section~\ref{sec:obj}.
perturbations.perturbed: Because absolute growth scores are noisy and platform-dependent, our objectives consider both quantitative agreement and the relative ordering of growth across media. In particular, we map measured growth scores to heuristic target biomass production rates by calibrating against the medium with the lowest observed growth, as described in Section~\ref{sec:obj}.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text states calibration is done against the medium with the lowest observed growth, but the original methodology specifies calibrating against the medium with the highest observed growth to set target rates.

perturbations.original: During both FBA and conventional culturing, from which OD and Biolog scores are derived, certain metabolites are made available at specific concentrations (culture medium), while others are produced at the end of reaction chains (end-products) or directed to the production of biomass.
perturbations.perturbed: During both FBA and conventional culturing, from which OD and Biolog scores are derived, certain metabolites are made available at specific concentrations (culture medium), while others are consumed at the end of reaction chains (end-products) or directed to the production of biomass.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation states end-products are 'consumed' at the end of reaction chains, which is biologically and logically contradictory to the definition of an 'end-product' (which is produced/secreted).

perturbations.original:
\section{The gap-filling problem}
\label{sec:gapfill}
perturbations.perturbed:
\section{The gap-filling solution}
\label{sec:gapfill}
perturbations.why_wrong: The section title is changed to 'The gap-filling solution', but the subsequent text and section label (sec:gapfill) clearly define the section as describing the 'problem' and the need to augment GEMs.

perturbations.original: GEMs assembled from genome annotations can miss reactions due to incomplete or uncertain gene--function associations. \emph{Gap-filling} aims to augment a GEM's reaction set with additional predefined reactions so that simulated growth better agrees with experimental observations across media. This is done by extending FBA to explore which additional reactions better contribute to such objective.
perturbations.perturbed: GEMs assembled from genome annotations can miss reactions due to incomplete or uncertain gene--function associations. \emph{Gap-filling} aims to reduce a GEM's reaction set by removing predefined reactions so that simulated growth better agrees with experimental observations across media. This is done by extending FBA to explore which additional reactions better contribute to such objective.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation claims gap-filling aims to 'reduce' the reaction set by 'removing' reactions, which contradicts the definition of gap-filling as 'augmenting' or 'adding' reactions to fill gaps.

perturbations.original: In this work, candidate reactions are drawn from AGORA2 \citep{Hein20AGORA2}. Each candidate reaction $i$ is assigned a cost $RC_i$ reflecting its plausibility for the target organisms, computed from taxonomic distance (Appendix~\ref{app:tax}); lower cost reactions are supported by closer relatives and are preferred.
perturbations.perturbed: In this work, candidate reactions are drawn from AGORA2 \citep{Hein20AGORA2}. Each candidate reaction $i$ is assigned a cost $RC_i$ reflecting its plausibility for the target organisms, computed from taxonomic distance (Appendix~\ref{app:tax}); higher cost reactions are supported by closer relatives and are preferred.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation states 'higher cost' reactions are supported by closer relatives and preferred, which contradicts the logic of optimization where lower costs represent higher plausibility and preference.
